topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell chimed, a jarring sound in the otherwise silent apartment. I dragged myself to the door, my limbs feeling heavy, like lead weights had settled in my joints. A package sat on the mat—a plain brown box, taped shut with rough, unfamiliar packing tape. It was clearly addressed to the previous occupant. A sigh escaped my lips, a long, drawn-out sound that seemed to vibrate the stale air of the hallway. I barely managed to sign for it, my hand trembling slightly as I processed the intrusion. I didn't have the energy to wonder what it was, or who might have sent it. I just wanted it gone, tucked away somewhere dark and forgotten, so I could go back to the blessed quiet of my own exhaustion.

[story 2]

I stared at the delivery notification on my phone, the screen illuminating the deep shadows under my eyes. Another delivery. Another piece of mail addressed to someone who wasn't me. My shoulders slumped further as I shuffled to the entryway. The box felt alien in my hands, heavier than it looked. "Oh, for heaven's sake," I muttered to myself, the words raspy from disuse. I barely registered the contents; my focus was entirely on the immediate task of disposal. I took the package, feeling a profound reluctance to engage with anything that demanded attention, and carried it toward the recycling bin, hoping the act of discarding it would somehow lighten the immense pressure building behind my sternum.

[story 3]

The sound of the delivery truck fading was the only announcement that something new had arrived. I leaned against the doorframe, letting my head drop back against the cool wood. Every muscle in my body felt sluggish, responding with a slow, deliberate inertia. The package lay there, an unwanted artifact from someone else's tenancy. I picked it up with minimal effort, my fingers brushing against the cardboard with a detached curiosity. A wave of profound inertia washed over me; the sheer effort of existing seemed monumental. I didn't bother unpacking it. Instead, I just nudged it toward the corner, a silent acknowledgment that this small responsibility was another burden I simply couldn't muster the strength to carry further.
